comments.text: Aristotle from Harmonic found a disproof of the statement at the Formal Conjectures project, due to a misformalization. Type-check it online! I don't know whether I will keep posting comments like this as more misformalizations are inevitably found, but I think I would like to draw some attention to (a) how frequent misformalization is and (b) the fact that tools like Aristotle can help find some such issues automatically, including in cases with nontrivial arguments.

I say keep going--these are really valuable to document. Misformalisations are (regrettably) not an edge case here; they’re a persistent, inherent risk whenever we translate informal conjectures into Lean. Especially at scale. Having concrete examples where Aristotle (or similar tools) disprove a misformalized statement is essential to building our trust in these pipelines; and especially to harden the formal conjectures repo over time.

I will keep submitting issues to the Formal Conjectures project, but probably commenting on a forum page here only for misformalizations of interest. For example, today Aristotle found that n=0 was mistakenly allowed in parts of [366], and that's not very interesting at all.

Using the deep research tool of ChatGPT I managed to find a few references I was not aware of previously, and I think my first comment here is mostly obsolete. Oh well. Long comment incoming! And by the way, as far as I know, in everything that follows the original problem with constant $\gamma = 2$ is considered, unless otherwise stated. Okay, to start off, N. Hegyvári, Some remarks on a problem of Erdős and Graham, Acta Math. Hung., Volume 53 (1989), 149-154 proves exactly Proposition 1 in my comment above; if $\alpha$ is a dyadic rational and $\beta$ is not a dyadic rational, then the sequence under consideration is complete. On the other hand, using $\alpha \ge 2$ and $\beta = 2^l \alpha$ with $l \in \mathbb{N}$, he also shows that the set of pairs $(\alpha, \beta)$ for which the sequence is not complete has the cardinality of the continuum. Even though I cannot access this paper by Hegyvári, I can find his follow-up paper where he mentions that he had obtained the dyadic rational result: N. Hegyvári, On complete sequences, Annales Univ. Sci. Budapest, Volume 34 (1991), 7-10. In this second paper (which you can find here, pages 7-10) he furthermore proves that, for any fixed $\alpha > 0$, the set of $\beta$ for which the corresponding sequence is complete either has Lebesgue measure $0$, or has infinite measure. In order to prove this he makes use of the observation in my first comment that $P(2\alpha, \beta)$ implies $P(\alpha, \beta)$. But he didn't stop there! N. Hegyvári, On sumset of certain sets, Publ. Math. Debrecen, Volume 45 (1994), 115-122 which can be found here, shows that the set of pairs $(\alpha, \beta)$ for which the sequence does not even contain an infinite arithmetic progression, also has the cardinality of the continuum. This strengthens the second result from the first paper I mentioned. Both cardinality continuum results make use of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ with $\frac{\beta}{\alpha}$ a power of two (i.e. rational), however. So they don't disprove the conjecture at hand. Then, much more recently, we finally have the following two papers X. W. Jiang, W. X. Ma, A conjecture of Hegyvári, Int. J. Number Theory, Volume 20 (2024), 915–933. J. H. Fang, J. Y. He, On a problem of Erdős and Graham, Acta Math. Hung., Volume 175 (2025), 532-542. Thanks to Terence who kindly sent me a copy of the second paper (and see his comment below as well!), both papers seem to extend Hegyvári's non-completeness results in the case that $\frac{\alpha}{\beta}$ is a power of two, but for $\alpha < 2$ and $\beta$ sufficiently large. Long story short, I believe the following four results for specific $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are known: Proposition 1. If exactly one of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ is a dyadic rational, then the sequence is complete. Proposition 2. If $\min(\alpha, \beta) < 2$ and $\max(\alpha, \beta) < 3$, then the sequence is complete. Proposition 3. If $\alpha \ge 2$ and $\beta = 2^l \alpha$ with $l \in \mathbb{N}$, then the sequence is not complete. Proposition 4. If $\alpha < 2$ and $\beta = 2^l \alpha$ with $l \in \mathbb{N}$ sufficiently large, then the sequence is not complete. Here, Propositions 1 and 3 are due to Hegyvári, while Proposition 4 is due to Jiang-Ma and Fang-He. Long story even shorter, as far as I can tell there is no solution to the main problem yet. On the other hand, I do suspect that the first paper by Hegyvári either settles the case $\gamma = \sqrt{2}$ or perhaps even $1 < \gamma \le \sqrt{2}$ (possibly implicitly). If anyone has access to this paper and could check, that would be appreciated. And in other news, the hypothesis that $\frac{\alpha}{\beta}$ is irrational can most likely be slightly weakened (if that wasn't clear already); Hegyvári conjectures that the sequence is complete if either $\alpha$ or $\beta$ is not a dyadic rational, and $\beta \neq 2^l \alpha$. In other words, Propositions 3 and 4 are conjecturally close to optimal. The result I mentioned in my second post in this comment section (namely, that if we change all floor functions to ceiling functions, then the sequence is complete, as long as either $\alpha$ or $\beta$ is not a dyadic rational) does seem to be new however, so perhaps I actually will keep working on my own paper on this subject. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
If it makes you feel any better, I had a fairly similar experience with #243, though with Vjeko playing the role of an AI deep research tool in that case.
That does make me feel better Terence, thank you ;)

I was able to get a hold of the Fang-He paper, but not the Jiang-Ma paper. From what I can tell: * Jiang-Ma showed that completeness fails when $1 < \alpha < 2$ and $\beta = 2^l \alpha$ for a sufficiently large $l$. In fact they prove something stronger than non-completeness but it is more technical to state. * Fang-He reprove this statement with a simpler proof and say even more about the set of subset sums. However they don't seem to claim anything about the case when $\beta/\alpha$ is not a power of two.

At a first glance, it seems like there should be no fundamental difference in this question if we change all floor functions to ceiling functions, right? A priori there seems to be no reason why proving the completeness for one should be any easier or harder than for the other. Well, as fate would have it, I can resolve this problem.. But only if we use the ceiling function! Isn't that remarkable? To elaborate on this, let me define two notions. We say that a sequence $S = (s_1, s_2, \ldots)$ is a $\Sigma$-sequence if a constant $c$ exists such that $s_{n+1} - \sum_{i=1}^n s_i \le c$ for all $n$. And we call a sequence precomplete if, for every $k \in \mathbb{N}$, there are $k$ consecutive integers that can all be written as the sum of distinct elements of $S$. In his $1964$ paper 'Complete Sequences of Polynomial Values' (see here), Graham proved that if $A = (a_1, a_2, \ldots)$ is a $\Sigma$-sequence and $B = (b_1, b_2, \ldots)$ is precomplete, then the union $(a_1, b_1, a_2, b_2, \ldots)$ is complete. Now, if $a_{n+1} \le 2a_n$ for all large enough $n$, then one can show that $A$ is a $\Sigma$-sequence. And the equality $a_{n+1} \le 2a_n$ is satisfied for all large enough $n$ if $a_n = \lceil 2^{n-1} \alpha \rceil$, but not if $a_n = \lfloor 2^{n-1} \alpha \rfloor$! Moreover, to prove that $B = (b_1, b_2, \ldots)$ is precomplete for $b_n = \lceil 2^{n-1} \beta \rceil$, it turns out that it is sufficient to show that there are just two consecutive integers that can both be written as the sum of distinct elements of $B$. And for $b_n = \lceil 2^{n-1} \beta \rceil$ one can check that, unless $\beta$ is a dyadic rational, there is some $n$ such that $b_1 + b_2 + \ldots + b_{n-1}$ is equal to $b_n - 1$, finishing the proof. All in all I can prove the following theorem: as long as either $\alpha$ or $\beta$ is not a dyadic rational, then $(\lceil \alpha \rceil, \lceil \beta\rceil, \lceil 2\alpha \rceil, \lceil 2\beta\rceil, \lceil 4\alpha \rceil, \lceil 4\beta\rceil, \ldots)$ is complete. For a (first version of a) full write-up, see here. I plan to combine this write-up with another project I'm working on, and will submit a proper paper to the arXiv in the coming months. I'll make sure to link it once it's finished. In the mean time, perhaps someone else can use some of these ideas to put the ball into the back of the net ;) As it does feel like we're very close to solving this problem. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

This change of ceilings and floors may lead to no difference in result, but proving it can be of substantial different difficulty. (I know an analogue of an olympiad question where this was the case e.g.)

Let $P(\alpha, \beta)$ denote the statement that the above sequence is complete, where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are assumed to be positive reals. Then I can prove the following two propositions: Proposition 1. If $\alpha = \frac{m}{2^n}$ is a dyadic rational (so in particular if $\alpha \in \mathbb{N}$) and $\beta$ is not a dyadic rational, then $P(\alpha, \beta)$ holds. Proposition 2. If $\min(\alpha, \beta) < 2$ and $\max(\alpha, \beta) < 3$, then $P(\alpha, \beta)$ holds. In general we see that $P(2\alpha, \beta)$ implies $P(\alpha, \beta)$, as the corresponding sequence for the former is a subsequence of the latter. In particular, to prove the first proposition it is sufficient to assume $\alpha \in \mathbb{N}$. So assume $\alpha = m \in \mathbb{N}$ and let us first note that the sequence $(\lfloor \alpha \rfloor, \lfloor 2\alpha \rfloor, \lfloor 4\alpha \rfloor, \ldots)$ then generates $m\mathbb{N}$. We therefore need to show that sums of distinct elements from $B = (\lfloor \beta \rfloor, \lfloor 2\beta \rfloor, \lfloor 4\beta \rfloor, \ldots)$ attain all residue classes modulo $m$. Now, for every prime divisor $p$ of $m$, I claim that $B$ contains infinitely many elements not divisible by $p$, if $\beta$ is not a dyadic rational. Indeed, if $p$ divides $\lfloor 2^l \beta \rfloor$ for some $l$, let $r \in \mathbb{N}$ and $\epsilon \in (0, 1)$ be such that $2^l \beta = pr + \epsilon$ and choose $l'$ such that $1 \le 2^{l'} \epsilon < 2$. We then get that $\left \lfloor 2^{l+l'} \beta \right \rfloor = 2^{l'}pr + 1$ is not divisible by $p$. Let $b_1 \pmod{m}, b_2 \pmod{m}, \ldots, b_k \pmod{m}$ be those residue classes modulo $m$ that appear infinitely often in $B$. We remark that it is not necessarily the case that there exists an $i$ such that $\gcd(b_i, m) = 1$. However, we do have $\gcd(b_1, \ldots, b_k, m) = 1$ by the aforementioned claim on prime divisors $p$ of $m$. We therefore get by the existence of the Frobenius number that all residue classes modulo $m$ appear among sums of the $b_i$, which was what we needed to prove. As for the second proposition, note we may assume $\beta \ge \alpha > 1$; the first inequality by symmetry, and the second inequality by the aforementioned fact that $P(2\alpha, \beta)$ implies $P(\alpha, \beta)$. Now define, for $n \ge 1$, $s_{2n-1} = \lfloor 2^{n-1} \alpha \rfloor$ and $s_{2n} = \lfloor 2^{n-1} \beta \rfloor$. We then claim that $(s_1, s_2, \ldots)$ generates the entirety of $\mathbb{N}$, for which it is necessary and sufficient to show $s_{n+1} \le 1 + \sum_{i=1}^n s_i$ for all $n \ge 0$. This follows for $n = 0$ by the fact that $s_1 = \lfloor \alpha \rfloor = 1$, and it follows for $n = 1$ as $s_2 = \lfloor \beta \rfloor \le 2$. For $n \ge 2$ we get by induction \begin{align*} s_{n+1} &\le 2s_{n-1} + 1 \\ &\le 2 + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} s_i \\ &\le 1 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i. \end{align*} In fact, the proof of this second proposition immediately generalizes to $\gamma \in (1,2)$ instead of $2$, and it even works in some cases for $\gamma \le 3$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
